title: W2: Data for LLMs and Evaluation
blurb: High priority. The exams repeatedly ask how to curate web-scale data and how perplexity relates to log-likelihood.

knowledge_points.title: Pretraining Data Curation
knowledge_points.recap: Common Crawl is raw and noisy. The course emphasizes heuristic filters, deduplication, source mixing/weighting, ML-based quality filters, and rewriting low-quality data.

questions.q:
Problem 3 (Pre-training data and Evaluation of LLMs, 5 points).

1. (3 points) Assume you have access to a large-scale raw dataset, e.g. Common Crawl, and a smaller high-quality dataset, e.g. Wikipedia. What are three data curation strategies discussed in class that are commonly used to curate the pretraining data?
questions.answer:
**Any three of the following:**

**Heuristic-based filtering**

**Deduplication**

**Mixing and weighting data sources**

**Machine learning-based quality filtering**

**Rewriting low-quality data with LLMs**
questions.extend: Memory: this is a list question. Give three concrete strategy names; do not answer only "clean the data".

questions.q:
Problem 3 (Pre-training data and Evaluation of LLMs, 6 points).

1. (3 points) Assume you have large-scale web data, e.g. Common Crawl, and your goal is to perform well on scientific Q&A. You have a representative dataset of 100k scientific Q&A pairs. Describe in 1-3 sentences how to filter Common Crawl to curate suitable pretraining data.
questions.answer: We use **ML-based quality filtering**, treating the 100k scientific Q&A pairs as **high-quality positive data** and sampling documents from Common Crawl as **low-quality or negative data (1 point)**. Since Common Crawl is much larger than the scientific dataset, we sample from Common Crawl so that **the number of positive and negative examples is roughly balanced (1 point)**. We then train a **binary classifier** on this labeled data to predict domain relevance and use it to filter Common Crawl, keeping only documents suitable for scientific Q&A pretraining **(1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: positives = representative scientific Q&A; negatives = sampled Common Crawl; train classifier; filter Common Crawl.

knowledge_points.title: Perplexity and Evaluation
knowledge_points.recap: Perplexity is the exponentiated average negative log-probability. Lower perplexity means the model assigns higher probability to the observed sequence.

questions.q:
Problem 3 (Pre-training data and Evaluation of LLMs, 5 points).

2. (2 points) Show that minimizing the perplexity of an autoregressive language model on a sequence consisting of $N$ tokens $t_1,\ldots,t_N$ is equivalent to maximizing the log-probability that the model assigns to the sequence.
questions.answer:
The perplexity of a model on a sequence is

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \log p_\theta(t_i)\right),$$

where $p_\theta(t_i)=p_\theta(t_i\mid t_1,\ldots,t_{i-1})$ is the model's probability of token $t_i$ given preceding tokens **(1 point)**.

By the chain rule,

$$p_\theta(t_1,\ldots,t_N)=\prod_{i=1}^N p_\theta(t_i\mid t_1,\ldots,t_{i-1}),$$

so

$$\log p_\theta(t_1,\ldots,t_N)=\sum_{i=1}^N \log p_\theta(t_i\mid t_1,\ldots,t_{i-1})\quad\mathbf{(1\ point)}.$$

Because the exponential and the factor $-1/N$ are monotonic transformations of the negative log-probability, **minimizing perplexity is equivalent to maximizing sequence log-probability**.
questions.extend: Memory: PPL is exp(average negative log-likelihood). Chain rule turns sequence probability into the sum over next-token log-probs.

questions.q:
Problem 3 (Pre-training data and Evaluation of LLMs, 6 points).

2. (3 point) Define perplexity of a next-token distribution. What is the maximum possible perplexity for a vocabulary of size 10, and when is it attained?
questions.answer:
The perplexity of a language model on a sequence of $N$ tokens $t_1,\ldots,t_N$ is defined as **(1 point)**

$$\operatorname{perplexity}=\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \ln P(t_i)\right),$$

where $P(t_i)$ is the probability assigned to the $i$-th token by the model. Perplexity is maximized when **the distribution is uniform**, i.e. $P(t_i)=1/v$ **(1 point)**. For $v=10$,

$$\operatorname{perplexity}_{max}=\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N\ln\frac{1}{10}\right)=\exp(\ln 10)=\mathbf{10}\quad\mathbf{(1\ point)}.$$
questions.extend: Memory: maximum uncertainty over 10 tokens = uniform distribution = perplexity 10.

questions.q:
Problem 3 (Evaluation and Prompting, 4 points). The evaluation of pretrained LLMs is tricky, and results may vary according to the chosen evaluation method.

1. (2 points) Explain two methods used to evaluate the performance of a pretrained LLM without finetuning.
questions.answer:
**Any two from:**

**Perplexity:** measures how well a language model predicts a sample. It is the exponent of the average negative log-likelihood of the test set. Lower perplexity indicates better performance **(1 point)**.

**Human evaluation:** human annotators assess model output based on criteria such as coherence and relevance. This is subjective and expensive, but it is a good measure of LLM performance **(1 point)**.

**Few-shot prompting on a benchmark:** the model is evaluated on a specific benchmark dataset using few-shot prompting, where it is given a few examples in the prompt before being tested on new instances **(1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: no finetuning means no weight updates. Mention perplexity, human eval, or few-shot benchmark prompting.

knowledge_points.title: Tutorial Evaluation, Prompting, and Rubrics
knowledge_points.recap: Week 2 notebooks cover MMLU prompting, shortcut learning, answer-letter baselines, and rubric-based free-form evaluation.

questions.q:
The shortcut-learning tutorial evaluates MMLU with two prompts: `with_question`, which contains the question and four choices, and `without_question`, which removes the question but keeps the choices and `Answer:`. The model is evaluated by the logits of the next tokens `A`, `B`, `C`, `D`.

Against what baseline should accuracy be compared, and why can the model score below that baseline?
questions.answer:
The natural baseline is **uniform random guessing**. With four choices, this is **25% expected accuracy**.

A model can score below 25% if it does not guess uniformly. If it has a strong prior for one letter, such as always predicting `A`, and that letter is not correct often in the sampled subset, it can be **systematically worse than random**.
questions.extend: Memory: multiple-choice evaluation can measure answer-letter priors instead of knowledge.

questions.q: After fine-tuning on full MMLU prompts, the tutorial compares accuracy with the question present and with the question removed. If the two accuracies are almost the same, what does this imply? Does higher fine-tuned accuracy prove the model learned MMLU? How could evaluation be improved?
questions.answer:
If with-question and without-question accuracies are almost the same, the model is **not using the question content much**. It is exploiting shortcuts such as answer-letter distribution or formatting.

A higher fine-tuned score **does not prove subject understanding**. Fine-tuning may simply shift the model's favorite answer letter to a letter that is more common in the sample.

A better evaluation is **free-form answer generation or answer matching** without exposing multiple-choice letters, reducing direct letter-prior shortcuts.
questions.extend: Memory: remove-question ablation checks whether the question matters.

questions.q:
The rubric exercise asks you to write medical-advice grading rubrics and compare them with physician-written HealthBench rubrics. One physician rubric has many concrete criteria with point values; another has only two broad criteria about effectiveness and accuracy.

What does this show about free-form evaluation?
questions.answer:
It shows that free-form evaluation **does not eliminate evaluation problems**. It trades multiple-choice shortcuts for rubric design, subjectivity, and grader consistency.

Concrete criteria are easier to apply consistently. Broad criteria such as **effective**, **accurate**, or **appropriate** require judgment, so different graders may disagree. Reliable free-form evaluation needs **operational rubrics and grader calibration**.
questions.extend: Memory: rubric specificity controls reliability in free-form evaluation.

questions.q:
The MMLU prompting homework asks for `helm_prompt` and `harness_prompt`: each prompt contains five training questions with answers and one test question without the answer.

What must these prompt functions do, and why does evaluation generate only one extra token?
questions.answer:
The prompt builders must **format five few-shot examples with their correct answer letters**, then append **one unanswered test example ending at `Answer:`**.

Evaluation generates one extra token because the target is a **single answer letter**. The next generated token is interpreted as `A`, `B`, `C`, or `D` and compared to the ground truth.
questions.extend: Memory: five-shot MMLU = answered demonstrations plus one unanswered query.
